era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the Hundred Years' War in its final phase, with French forces gaining momentum against English occupation. The Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia. The Italian Renaissance is in its early stages, with Florence and other city-states experiencing cultural and economic flourishing. Ming China under the Xuande Emperor is consolidating administrative control. The Ottoman Empire is expanding in the Balkans. This is a period of religious reformation, military innovation, and cultural renaissance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
Southeast Asia (limited chronicle material in Western sources)
Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse documentation)

events.title: Joan of Arc's military victories; Relief of Orléans
events.region: Orléans, Loire Valley, Kingdom of France
events.description: In 1429, Joan of Arc led French forces to a series of military victories that marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War. Most notably, she participated in the relief of Orléans, which had been under English siege. The French victory at Orléans on 8 May 1429 was a major morale boost and demonstrated that English forces could be defeated. Joan's subsequent victories at Jargeau (12 June), Meung-sur-Loire (15 June), and Beaugency (17 June) further weakened English control of the Loire Valley. These victories led to the coronation of Charles VII at Reims on 17 July 1429, legitimizing his claim to the French throne. Joan's role in these victories is debated by historians; some emphasize her military leadership, while others argue she was primarily a symbolic figurehead whose presence boosted morale.
key_figures: Charles VII (King of France, crowned at Reims)
Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

sources.name: English government records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The major battles and Joan's participation are confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The dates of the battles are well-established. The significance of Joan's role is debated, but the events themselves are confirmed.

events.title: Coronation of Charles VII at Reims
events.description: On 17 July 1429, Charles VII was crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral. This coronation was of immense symbolic and political significance. It legitimized Charles's claim to the French throne against the English-backed Henry VI and marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War. The coronation was made possible by Joan of Arc's military victories, which had opened the route to Reims. The ceremony itself followed traditional French coronation rituals and was attended by French nobility. The coronation boosted French morale and international recognition of Charles VII's legitimacy.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (military leader who enabled the coronation)
events.certainty_note: The coronation date of 17 July 1429 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The ceremony and its significance are well-documented.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages; sixth voyage returns
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: The sixth of Zheng He's treasure voyages returned to China in 1429 after departing in 1421. These massive expeditions, involving hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The voyages were primarily diplomatic and trade missions, establishing tributary relationships and conducting trade. By 1429, the political climate in China was shifting; the Yongle Emperor, who had sponsored the voyages, had died in 1424. His successors were less enthusiastic about maritime expansion, and the voyages would cease after 1435. The return of the sixth voyage in 1429 marked the beginning of the end of this era of Chinese maritime exploration.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, less supportive than his predecessor)

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: The return of the sixth voyage in 1429 is attested in Ming records, though the exact date may vary slightly depending on the source. The broader context of the voyages is confirmed.

events.title: Hussite Wars; continued conflict in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars continued in 1429 with ongoing conflict between Hussite factions (Taborites and Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The year saw various military campaigns and skirmishes, though no single decisive battle. The Hussite movement, which had begun as a religious reform movement following the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, had evolved into a complex military and political conflict. By 1429, the Hussite forces had demonstrated their military effectiveness through innovations in wagon-fortress tactics and the use of firearms. The conflict would continue until the Compacts of Prague in 1436.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)
Jan Žižka (Hussite commander, died 1424 but legacy dominant)
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Hussite Wars in 1429 is confirmed by contemporary sources. Specific battles and campaigns are documented, though the exact chronology may vary.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; expansion along African coast
events.description: Prince Henry of Portugal (1394–1460) continued to sponsor maritime expeditions along the African coast in 1429. Portuguese ships were systematically exploring the West African coast, seeking gold, spices, and other valuable commodities. The expeditions were also motivated by religious zeal (crusade against Islam) and the desire to establish trading posts. By 1429, Portuguese explorers had reached the coast of what is now Senegal and Guinea. These early expeditions laid the groundwork for the Portuguese slave trade, which would intensify in the following decades.
key_figures: Henry the Navigator (Prince of Portugal, patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (mid-15th century, but based on contemporary records)
Portuguese royal records and navigation logs

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1429 is attested in later chronicles and cartographic evidence. Specific expeditions in 1429 are less precisely dated than the broader context of exploration.

disconfirming_evidence: The role of Joan of Arc in the military victories of 1429 is heavily debated by modern historians. While her participation is confirmed, the extent of her military leadership versus her symbolic value is disputed. Some scholars argue that the victories were primarily due to French commanders like Jean de Dunois and that Joan's presence was exploited for propaganda purposes. The coronation of Charles VII at Reims, while symbolically important, did not immediately end the Hundred Years' War; the conflict continued for another 24 years. The characterization of Zheng He's voyages as 'peaceful exploration' obscures their role in Ming tributary diplomacy and the coercive aspects of 'gift-giving' to foreign rulers. The Hussite Wars are often portrayed as a conflict between 'reformers' and 'conservatives,' but this oversimplifies the complex factional divisions within the Hussite movement itself. Portuguese exploration is often presented as a precursor to the Age of Discovery, but this teleological framing obscures the fact that Portuguese expansion was driven primarily by commercial and religious motives, not by a desire to 'discover' new lands.
historiographic_note: The year 1429 is well-documented for Western Europe (Joan of Arc's campaigns are extensively recorded) and moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive). The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Joan of Arc's campaigns are recorded from multiple perspectives (French, English, Burgundian), but each source reflects the biases of its origin. The French sources tend to emphasize Joan's role, while English sources downplay it. Ming records are official court documents that emphasize imperial achievements. Portuguese exploration records are fragmentary and often compiled retrospectively. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year. The dominance of European sources in modern historical training means that events in other regions are likely underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Joan's military victories in 1429 enabled the coronation of Charles VII at Reims
Joan's military campaigns in 1429 led to her capture in 1430
Charles VII's coronation in 1429 was a step toward the eventual French victory in the Hundred Years' War
The return of the sixth voyage in 1429 was part of the sequence leading to the cessation of voyages in 1435
The Hussite Wars of 1429 led to the Compacts of Prague in 1436
